name: Liu Bei
description: Liu Bei, also known as Emperor Zhaolie of Han, is a significant figure in Chinese mythology and history, particularly revered in Taoism and folk religion. He is traditionally considered the founder of the Shu Han dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE). While a historical figure, Liu Bei's image has been significantly mythologized over centuries, transforming him into a benevolent and virtuous ruler embodying Confucian ideals of righteousness, benevolence, and filial piety. He is often depicted as a compassionate leader dedicated to restoring the Han dynasty and alleviating the suffering of the common people. Popular depictions portray him with exaggerated features of kindness and wisdom, solidifying his status as a deity-like figure. His role in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms further solidified his legendary status, cementing his place in the pantheon of popular Chinese deities and cultural heroes.
subtitle: Emperor Zhaolie of Han

keyMyths.title: The Oath of the Peach Garden
keyMyths.description: This legendary event recounts the sworn brotherhood between Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei. They pledged allegiance to each other, vowing to work together to restore peace and prosperity to the land. This oath symbolizes their unwavering loyalty and commitment to justice, becoming a cornerstone of Liu Bei's heroic image and a powerful symbol of brotherhood in Chinese culture. The peach garden itself is often depicted as a sacred space where divine bonds are forged.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 1

keyMyths.title: Zhuge Liang's Allegiance
keyMyths.description: The story of Liu Bei's three visits to the thatched cottage of Zhuge Liang is a testament to his wisdom and humility. Despite his imperial lineage, Liu Bei patiently sought the counsel of the reclusive strategist. Zhuge Liang's acceptance of Liu Bei's offer marked a turning point, providing him with the strategic genius necessary to establish the Shu Han dynasty. This act solidified Liu Bei's reputation as a discerning and virtuous leader.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 38

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Changban
keyMyths.description: During the chaotic retreat from Changban, Liu Bei demonstrated his unwavering commitment to his people. Despite the overwhelming odds, he refused to abandon the civilians who followed him. This act of selflessness, even at the risk of his own life and the safety of his family, cemented his image as a compassionate ruler who placed the well-being of his subjects above all else. This battle is often depicted as a moment of divine intervention protecting Liu Bei.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 42

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel that greatly romanticizes and mythologizes Liu Bei's life and deeds.
sources.text: A historical novel that greatly romanticizes and mythologizes Liu Bei's life and deeds.

sources.author: Rafe de Crespigny
sources.work: A Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to the Three Kingdoms (23-220 AD)
